title: Spec-Driven Development Deep Dive — Part 3: Intent Infrastructure and the Future of SDD
description: Will the evolution of memory capabilities in large language models (LLMs) end SDD? This article explores the development of autonomous agents after solving the problem of context decay, revealing the final form and irreplaceable nature of SDD as an intent layer infrastructure.
content:
# Spec-Driven Development Deep Dive — Part 3: Intent Infrastructure and the Future of SDD

**Will the Evolution of Large Language Model (LLM) Memory Eliminate SDD?** With the maturation of agent working memory management, the problem of cross-session knowledge forgetting will be eliminated by technology. However, in this evolution, Spec-Driven Development will not only not disappear, but will eventually evolve into the "core intent layer infrastructure" of software development due to the increasing demands for business decision security and intent accuracy in systems.

> **TL;DR**: Will LLM internally "solve" the problems of SDD? Partially, but not entirely. This article argues that with the rise of agent memory management, context decay will be gradually smoothed out by technology, but because the system's requirements for "intent accuracy" will increase in the opposite direction, SDD will eventually evolve into the intent layer infrastructure of software development.

**Series Navigation:**

- [Part 1: The Essence and Challenges of SDD](/posts/sdd-series-part-1-evolution/)

- [Part 2: GitHub Spec Kit Practical Guide](/posts/sdd-series-part-2-spec-kit/)

- **Part 3 (This Post): Intent Layer Infrastructure and the Future of SDD**

- [Part 4: Building a Simple SDD Workflow Using GitHub Issues](/posts/sdd-series-part-4-github-issues/)

![Minimalist Intent Layer Infrastructure Diagram](/assets/img/post/sdd-series/sdd-future-minimalist.png)

*Illustration: From Developer-Written Documentation to a Completely Code-Free Autonomous Intent Core*

## Facing the Deep Pain: Will LLM Make SDD Unnecessary?

Many people wonder: if future intelligent agents possess infinitely long memories and extraordinary reasoning abilities, will we still need to manually write lengthy specification documents?

The conclusion is: **Some problems will be solved, but the core significance will not be weakened.**

The advancement of LLM capabilities and the evolution of SDD methodologies are not simple replacements, but rather a symbiotic evolution—the stronger the model's ability to generate code, the higher the system's requirements for "precise expression of intent" and "secure control of intent."

## What LLM Technology Evolution Can Solve

### Context Decay Is About to Be Overcome by Technology

Whether it's the lack of architectural context or the interruption of development intent midway, how to maintain a highly consistent development progress of the Agent across multiple dialogue windows is currently the most troublesome problem for engineers. However, judging from breakthroughs in industry and academia, it's only a matter of time before technology solves it.

For example, in its latest engineering practice disclosure, Anthropic demonstrated a two-phase solution: the "initialization agent" builds a temporary sandbox environment and reads the specification during its first run; subsequently, the "coding agent" incrementally advances modifications in each session, ultimately producing a clean state package "without obvious bugs and with documentation comments" for the next stage to handle. This is essentially an internalized, automated SDD logic.

Academia is also driving the evolution of agent memory models, dividing memory into factual memory, experiential memory, and working memory.

**Evolution Conclusion**: Conservatively estimated, by 2026-2027, there will be a significant application-level breakthrough in the context persistence problem. SDD documentation maintenance will shift from "developers manually tracking and managing Markdown files" to "a memory layer automatically built by the agent in the background." At that time, the cost of SDD documentation maintenance and organization will be greatly reduced.

## The Unresolved Aspects of LLM Technology Evolution

### Precision of Intent Expression: An Inescapable Bottom Line of Contradiction

Even if the Agent can automatically maintain working memory at extremely low cost, SDD still requires explicit human intervention using a domain-specific language to describe the highest-level business intent, rather than letting AI arbitrarily decide on the underlying data table selection.

The emphasis we made in the previous two articles on building highly standardized acceptance criteria (like the Given/When/Then syntax in BDD) is to prevent all business designs from losing their way under the unified logic of the model. As the scale and throughput of AI code generation far exceed the limits of human visual review, the shortest plank in software delivery is no longer "how fast and how correct the code is," but rather "how to effectively communicate our subjective trade-offs."

### The "Correctness" of Intent Cannot Be Replaced by Automation

A valuable Spec is not merely a dry, machine-executed contract. It is the final expression of the human development team's decision-making power regarding the overall architectural purpose, risk tolerance limits, and compromises.

We can identify whether an engineering system has deviated from initial requirements by setting up automation tools (such as powerful Drift Detection) in the CI process. However, cold machine code cannot determine for itself whether such drift is "a stupid flaw" or "a concession made to compromise performance."

**High-level execution actions can be automated to an unlimited extent, but business significance and decision-making power belong entirely to humans.**

## The Three-Stage Maturation Evolution Path of SDD

Combined with the current rapid development of artificial intelligence, we believe that the gradual deepening and evolution of SDD in the industry can be divided into the following three major development stages:

### Stage One (Current to 2026): A Major Explosion of Tooling and Standard Convergence

Recently, more than 450 different SDD-related kits have sprung up in the industry (such as the GitHub Spec Kit mentioned in the previous article). The primary task at this stage is "standardization and noise reduction," whether through connecting various large models via [MCP (Model Context Protocol)](/posts/mcp-apps-guide/) or by leveraging a unified underlying file constraint paradigm. During this stage, numerous grassroots hacking practices have been transformed into standard operating procedures (SOPs) for formal enterprises.

### Stage Two (2026-2028): Mature Implementation of Spec-as-Source (Specific Code Homogenization)

Currently, most specifications on the market only function at the Spec-First guiding level (using only a single stream). However, at this stage, mainstream industries will truly transition to **Spec-as-Source** (completely making specifications the main logic source code).

When core enterprise applications need to undergo major iterative foundation upgrades or fix high-risk network dependencies, the underlying development process no longer requires manual code retrieval and testing. All basic functional code can rely entirely on the highest-level original specification documents, undergoing system-wide cross-validation and automatically building entirely new branch versions. This is the first step in the operation of an "autonomous system."

### Phase Three (2028+): The Final Separation of High-Level Strategic Intent and Underlying Technology

In this so-called final form of SDD, specification documents will transcend auxiliary references and directly become enforceable, executable runtime entities (i.e., architecture will not only be a product of the initial design phase but will also proactively control the entire system's runtime state as an invariant throughout the code lifecycle).

You write a highly logically rigorous domain-level specification. Your agent matrix can then intelligently and without additional prompts or intervention, create a high-concurrency Java system, a lightning-fast Rust client engine, or a nimble Go service layer. This finally frees top-level strategic design from the shackles of syntax implementation.

## Conclusion

Fundamentally, software development is a recurring, ever-evolving problem: **between the vague and chaotic human intention and the precise, monotonous machine operating instructions, there will always be an indispensable translation and transformation layer.** **
**The translation and conversion layer was originally called C++, then Python, and now it has manifested as a Markdown format specification guide. Looking ahead ten years, it may evolve into some kind of highly abstract intelligent negotiation protocol.

In the foreseeable future, the SDD methodology will not be abruptly swept away by the surging LLM wave. Instead, it will subtly integrate into every corner of daily development, evolving into an "intent infrastructure" that operates autonomously at the machine level, with only macro-level judgments and corrections made by humans.

Tools will eventually disappear when the tide recedes, but this rigorous technical methodology of standards guiding everything will endure.
